input_candidates.truth_value: Figure 2a presents engineering stress-strain curves of Fe-26Mn-6Al-1C steel at different solution temperatures (1000°C, 1100°C, 1200°C), but exact numerical values cannot be extracted from the text-only caption.
input_candidates.prediction_value: TEM observations indicated only fundamental reflections in 3Al and 6Al steels, but weak superlattice spots in 10Al steel and strong ones in 12Al steel, confirming the presence of ordered κ-(Fe,Mn)3AlCx carbides.

input_candidates.truth_value: Figure 2b plots yield strength vs. d^{-1/2} for 6Al steel, with data points approximately at d^{-1/2} = 0.14 μm^{-1/2} (340 MPa), 0.20 μm^{-1/2} (380 MPa), 0.26 μm^{-1/2} (450 MPa).
input_candidates.prediction_value: When twin boundaries were considered, the grain size of 10Al steel was 39 μm and that of 12Al steel was 16 μm.

input_candidates.truth_value: Figure 3 is a stacked bar chart showing the contributions of base strength, solid solution by Al, ferrite, grain refining, and carbides to the yield strength of 3Al, 6Al, 10Al, and 12Al steels; the exact numeric values are not provided in the text.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Engineering stress–strain data for 6Al steel at different solid solution temperatures are presented in Figure 2a (tabulated columns labeled 1000s, 1100s, 1200s), showing stress levels ranging from ~350–450 MPa at low strain to ~550–650 MPa at 60% strain depending on the treatment condition.

input_candidates.truth_value: Quantitative breakdown of yield strength increase in 12Al steel compared to 10Al (952 MPa vs. 470 MPa): solid solution ~203.11 MPa, grain refinement ~61.39 MPa, ferrite contribution ~50 MPa, and κ-carbide precipitation ~361.93 MPa. The precipitation strengthening effect is 1.78 times the solid solution hardening effect.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Hall–Petch experimental data for 6Al steel: yield strengths of 340 MPa, 380 MPa, and 450 MPa correspond to d^-1/2 values of 0.14, 0.20, and 0.26 μm^-1/2, respectively, yielding σ0 = 250.70 MPa and kG = 683.07 MPa·μm^1/2.

input_candidates.truth_value: The strain hardening behavior differs among steels: 3Al and 6Al exhibit obvious strain hardening (TWIP effect), 10Al and 12Al show reduced hardening; 12Al exhibits no remarkable strain hardening, attributed to shearable κ-carbides causing glide plane softening.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Estimated strengthening contributions from Figure 3 and text: 3Al — base 270 MPa, solid solution by Al 50 MPa; 6Al — base 260 MPa, solid solution by Al 80 MPa; 10Al — base 270 MPa, solid solution by Al 120 MPa, ferrite 20 MPa, grain refining 30 MPa, carbide 40 MPa; 12Al — base 270 MPa, solid solution by Al 190 MPa, ferrite 60 MPa, grain refining 130 MPa, carbide 350 MPa.
